response:
The error you are encountering stems from a fundamental mismatch between your .NET 7 cross-platform application and the WPF library, which historically relies on the .NET Framework 4.0 assembly identity. When you reference WPF in a .NET 7 project targeting Windows, the runtime expects the modern .NET Core implementation of WPF, but your code or a transitive dependency is explicitly requesting the legacy `PresentationFramework` version 4.0.0.0, causing the system to fail because that specific file does not exist in the .NET 7 runtime environment.

To resolve this without downgrading your main application to .NET Framework, you must ensure that your WPF project is configured as a .NET 7 project that targets the `net7.0-windows` framework identifier rather than a generic .NET Framework target. This configuration forces the compiler to use the modern WPF implementation that is fully compatible with .NET 7 on Windows, while allowing the project to be excluded from the build process entirely when the target platform is macOS, thus maintaining your cross-platform architecture.

cousin projects in your solution that reference the WPF assembly must be updated to use the `net7.0-windows` target to avoid pulling in legacy dependencies that expect the old 4.0 version. If you have separate class libraries or shared code that are currently targeting `net472` or similar .NET Framework versions, they will implicitly demand the old WPF assemblies, so you must migrate every component that touches WPF to the modern .NET 7 stack to ensure the assembly versioning aligns correctly.

You should also inspect your `csproj` files for any explicit `<Reference>` tags pointing to `PresentationFramework` or `System.Windows` that might be hardcoded to the 4.0 version, as these need to be removed or replaced with package references to the modern `Microsoft.NETCore.App` runtime. Additionally, verify that your build configuration does not have any legacy settings enabled that force the use of the full .NET Framework runtime instead of the .NET 7 runtime when building for Windows.

Finally, once you have standardized all WPF-related projects to target `net7.0-windows`, the `System.IO.FileNotFoundException` should disappear because the runtime will correctly locate the modern WPF assemblies bundled with .NET 7. This approach allows your application to continue using WPF on Windows and Cocoa on macOS without requiring a migration to the older .NET Framework, preserving the cross-platform capabilities of your .NET
